reasoning: The <script> tag is genuinely whitelisted in wp_kses_wf() at lines 501-507 of utility.php — that is a real misconfiguration. However, the finding's reachability claim is false. Exploitability requires attacker-controlled HTML containing a <script> element to reach wp_kses_wf(). Examination of all 8 call sites shows none permit this: (1) utility.php:100 — hardcoded 'checked=checked' string; (2) utility.php:126 — toggle-switch HTML from internal plugin options; (3) utility.php:179,199 — select/radio HTML from internal plugin arrays; (4) admin.php:128 — notice text from DB option WPCAPTCHA_NOTICES_KEY, populated only by add_notice() with hardcoded translated strings; (5) admin.php:353 — tab icon, hardcoded or filterable only by admins via wpcaptcha_tabs; (6) functions.php:786 — display_message embedding $wpcaptcha_recovery_email which is passed through sanitize_email() at line 731, stripping angle brackets and preventing any tag injection; (7) wf-flyout.php:115 — CSS wrapped in <style> from config values already through sanitize_text_field(); (8) wf-flyout.php:164 — flyout HTML where labels use esc_html(), hrefs esc_url(), classes esc_attr(). No unauthenticated or low-privilege attacker can supply a <script> element to any of these paths. The whitelisted script tag is a code quality defect but not an exploitable XSS vulnerability given the actual data flows.
